title: Temperature & Sampling Parameters
concept: How temperature, top-p, and top-k reshape output diversity independent of the prompt text itself.

exercises.prompt: When A/B testing two prompt variants, what is the best way to determine which one is superior?

options.text: Ask the LLM which prompt it likes better.
Run both prompt variants on the same evaluation dataset and measure their performance against a clear metric.
Merge both prompts into one long instruction set to get the best of both worlds.

exercises.explain: A/B testing involves running two prompt versions against the exact same test dataset and using a concrete metric (like accuracy or user acceptance) to see which version performs better.

cards.title: Beyond the Prompt
cards.body: Have you ever noticed how the same prompt can sometimes give you a boring, predictable answer, and other times a wild, creative story? You don't always need to rewrite your prompt to change this; instead, you can turn the dial on the model's internal decision-making engine.

cards.title: What are Sampling Parameters?
cards.body: Sampling parameters like temperature, top-p, and top-k are settings that control how an LLM selects its next word. Instead of changing the prompt text, these parameters change the math behind how creative, random, or focused the model's choices are.

cards.title: The Word Jar analogy
cards.body: Imagine the LLM's brain is a giant jar of word marbles, where more common words sit at the top. High temperature shakes the jar hard so rare marbles bounce to the surface, while top-p and top-k act as a filter, removing all but the most colorful marbles before you choose.

cards.title: Temperature in Action
cards.body: If you ask a model to complete 'I want to eat...', a low temperature (0.1) will almost always output 'pizza' or 'apple'. A high temperature (0.9) opens the door to 'spaghetti', 'sushi', or even 'adventure'.

cards.title: The Creativity Myth
cards.body: Many think that raising temperature magically makes the model smarter or better at reasoning. In reality, it only increases randomness; if the model doesn't know a fact, raising the temperature will just make it hallucinate more creatively.

cards.title: Focus vs. Chaos
cards.body: Comparing low temperature (focused, safe) to high temperature (creative, unpredictable) when generating text.

left.text: Predictable, highly focused on the most probable words. Best for code and facts.

right.text: Diverse, unexpected word choices. Best for brainstorming and creative writing.

exercises.prompt: What is the main difference between the top-p and top-k sampling parameters?

options.text: Top-p changes the prompt text, while top-k changes the system instructions.
Top-p filters based on cumulative probability, while top-k filters based on a fixed number of words.
Top-p makes the model faster, while top-k makes the model consume less memory.
Top-p only allows nouns, while top-k only allows verbs.

exercises.explain: Top-p (nucleus sampling) limits the token pool to a cumulative probability percentage (like the top 90% most likely words), whereas top-k limits the pool to a fixed count (like the top 50 words).

exercises.prompt: True or False: Setting the temperature closer to 0 makes the model's outputs more deterministic and repetitive.
exercises.explain: Lowering the temperature to 0 makes the model choose the single most likely word every time, resulting in highly consistent and predictable answers.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence about sampling parameters.
exercises.sentence: Sampling parameters let you control the diversity of the response without editing the prompt.
exercises.explain: Adjusting sampling parameters changes the diversity of the output without modifying a single letter of the original prompt text.

exercises.prompt: Identify which of these instructions sounds most like it was generated by an AI assistant giving technical configuration instructions.

options.text: Adjust temperature to 0.7 and set top_p to 0.9 to optimize token distribution diversity.
Turn down the temperature if you want the model to stop making things up.
We can use top-k to limit the word choices to the top 40 most likely options.

exercises.explain: Option B uses natural human phrasing. Option C is human-like but slightly robotic. Option A uses highly structured, math-heavy LLM system prompt jargon to describe sampling parameters, which is classic AI-generated style.

exercises.prompt: If you set the temperature to a very high 1.5 but restrict top-k to exactly 1, how will the LLM behave?

options.text: It becomes completely random because the high temperature overrides the top-k filter.
It behaves deterministically, always selecting the single highest-probability token.
It throws an API error because temperature and top-k cannot be used together.
It only outputs empty spaces and punctuation because the filters cancel each other out.

exercises.explain: Setting top-k to 1 forces the model to pick only the single most probable next token at every step, creating a completely deterministic output regardless of temperature. Setting temperature to 0 achieves a similar effect, but combining a high temperature with top-k=1 still results in a single-path output because there are no other options in the pool to choose from.
